name: Income elasticity of labor supply
description: Percent change in annual hours worked from a 1 percent increase in non-labor income, holding the net-of-tax wage fixed and excluding participation responses.
population: Prime-age workers in the United States who are already working
preferred_interpretation: Intensive-margin annual-hours elasticity conditional on employment
benchmark_summary: Often modestly negative in review papers and lottery evidence.
benchmark_source: CBO 2012; Blundell and MaCurdy 1999; Imbens, Rubin, and Sacerdote 2001

name: Frisch elasticity of labor supply
description: Percent change in annual hours worked from a 1 percent change in the net-of-tax wage, holding the marginal utility of wealth constant and excluding participation responses.
population: Prime-age workers in the United States who are already working
preferred_interpretation: Intensive-margin annual-hours Frisch elasticity used in lifecycle or macro calibration
benchmark_summary: A standard calibrated labor-supply parameter in OG-USA and OG-Core style models.
benchmark_source: OG-USA macro calibration docs; OG-Core parameter docs; Altonji 1986; Peterman 2016

name: Uncompensated wage elasticity of labor supply
description: Percent change in annual hours worked from a 1 percent change in the net-of-tax wage, allowing both substitution and income effects and excluding participation responses.
population: Prime-age workers in the United States who are already working
preferred_interpretation: Intensive-margin uncompensated annual-hours elasticity
benchmark_summary: Relevant for mapping between substitution and income effects in labor-supply reviews.

name: Employment participation elasticity of single mothers
description: Percent change in employment or labor-force participation from a 1 percent change in the net-of-tax wage.
population: Single mothers in the United States
preferred_interpretation: Medium-run employment participation elasticity with respect to the net-of-tax wage
benchmark_summary: Participation responses can materially exceed intensive-margin hours responses.
benchmark_source: Eissa and Liebman 1996; extensive-margin labor-supply literature

name: Coefficient of relative risk aversion
description: CRRA coefficient in household utility over consumption.
population: Representative household or calibrated household block
preferred_interpretation: CRRA coefficient, often denoted sigma
benchmark_summary: A core household-preference parameter used in overlapping-generations models.
benchmark_source: OG-Core parameter docs; Attanasio, Banks, Meghir, and Weber 1999

name: Intertemporal elasticity of substitution
description: Percent change in the growth rate of nondurable consumption from a 1 percent change in the intertemporal price of consumption.
population: Representative U.S. household in an annual macro-calibration setting
preferred_interpretation: IES for nondurable consumption in annual macro calibration, distinct from risk aversion
benchmark_summary: A canonical macro quantity often discussed alongside risk aversion.

name: Annual discount factor
description: One-year time discount factor for household utility.
population: Representative household or calibrated household block
preferred_interpretation: Annual beta used in calibration
benchmark_summary: A standard preference parameter in OG-Core style models.

name: Elasticity of substitution between capital and labor
description: Percent change in the capital-labor ratio from a 1 percent change in the marginal rate of technical substitution between capital and labor in a CES production function.
population: U.S. aggregate private-sector production
preferred_interpretation: Long-run CES elasticity of substitution between capital and labor in aggregate private-sector production
benchmark_summary: A central production-side calibration parameter.

name: Capital share in production
description: Output elasticity with respect to capital in an aggregate production function.
population: U.S. aggregate production
preferred_interpretation: Cobb-Douglas capital share alpha
benchmark_summary: A baseline production parameter used in many macro models.
benchmark_source: National income accounts and macro calibration literature

name: Armington elasticity
description: Elasticity of substitution between the aggregate import composite and domestically produced goods at the top trade nest.
population: U.S. aggregate trade or CGE calibration
preferred_interpretation: Top-level import-versus-domestic Armington elasticity
benchmark_summary: A standard open-economy elasticity used in CGE and policy models.

name: Elasticity of taxable income
description: Percent change in taxable income from a 1 percent change in the net-of-tax rate.
population: High-income taxpayers in the United States
preferred_interpretation: Medium-run ETI for top earners with avoidance and real responses combined
benchmark_summary: A canonical sufficient statistic in public finance.
benchmark_source: Saez, Slemrod, and Giertz 2012; expert-elicitation and optimal-tax literature

name: Substitution elasticity of labor supply in a tax-benefit simulation
description: Response coefficient multiplying the bounded relative change in the effective marginal wage in a U.S. tax-benefit simulation. A 1 percent change in the effective marginal wage changes labor supply by this fraction. If set, this version applies uniformly across individuals.
population: Adults in the United States under a current-law tax-benefit simulation
preferred_interpretation: Global labor-supply substitution-response elasticity override used in a U.S. microsimulation model
benchmark_summary: Directly usable as the global labor-supply substitution-response override in PolicyEngine US.

name: Primary-earner substitution elasticity in a tax-benefit simulation, decile 1
description: Response coefficient multiplying the bounded relative change in the effective marginal wage for primary earners in earnings decile 1 in a U.S. tax-benefit simulation.
population: Primary earners in earnings decile 1 in the United States under a current-law tax-benefit simulation
preferred_interpretation: Primary-earner substitution-response elasticity for earnings decile 1 in a U.S. microsimulation model
benchmark_summary: Directly usable as the decile-specific primary-earner substitution parameter in PolicyEngine US.

description: Response coefficient multiplying the bounded relative change in the effective marginal wage for primary earners in earnings decile 2 in a U.S. tax-benefit simulation.

benchmark_summary: Directly usable as the decile-specific primary-earner substitution parameter in PolicyEngine US.

benchmark_summary: Directly usable as the decile-specific primary-earner substitution parameter in PolicyEngine US.

benchmark_summary: Directly usable as the decile-specific primary-earner substitution parameter in PolicyEngine US.

benchmark_summary: Directly usable as the decile-specific primary-earner substitution parameter in PolicyEngine US.

benchmark_summary: Directly usable as the decile-specific primary-earner substitution parameter in PolicyEngine US.

benchmark_summary: Directly usable as the decile-specific primary-earner substitution parameter in PolicyEngine US.

population: Primary earners in earnings decile 9 in the United States under a current-law tax-benefit simulation
preferred_interpretation: Primary-earner substitution-response elasticity for earnings decile 9 in a U.S. microsimulation model
benchmark_summary: Directly usable as the decile-specific primary-earner substitution parameter in PolicyEngine US.

name: Primary-earner substitution elasticity in a tax-benefit simulation, decile 10
description: Response coefficient multiplying the bounded relative change in the effective marginal wage for primary earners in earnings decile 10 in a U.S. tax-benefit simulation.
population: Primary earners in earnings decile 10 in the United States under a current-law tax-benefit simulation
preferred_interpretation: Primary-earner substitution-response elasticity for earnings decile 10 in a U.S. microsimulation model
benchmark_summary: Directly usable as the decile-specific primary-earner substitution parameter in PolicyEngine US.

name: Secondary-earner substitution elasticity in a tax-benefit simulation
description: Response coefficient multiplying the bounded relative change in the effective marginal wage for secondary earners in a U.S. tax-benefit simulation.
population: Secondary earners in the United States under a current-law tax-benefit simulation
preferred_interpretation: Secondary-earner substitution-response elasticity used in a U.S. microsimulation model
benchmark_summary: Directly usable as the secondary-earner substitution parameter in PolicyEngine US.

name: Capital gains realizations elasticity
description: Elasticity of long-term capital gains realizations with respect to the capital gains marginal tax rate in a U.S. tax model. A 1 percent increase in the capital-gains marginal tax rate changes realizations by this elasticity.
population: Individuals with long-term capital gains in the United States
preferred_interpretation: Medium-run elasticity of long-term capital gains realizations with respect to the capital-gains marginal tax rate
benchmark_summary: Directly usable as the capital-gains realizations response parameter in PolicyEngine US.

name: Capital gains realizations elasticity (net-of-tax-rate convention)
description: Elasticity of long-term capital gains realizations with respect to the net-of-tax rate (1 - tau) on capital gains. A 1 percent increase in the net-of-tax rate changes realizations by this elasticity. Sibling of tax.capital_gains_realizations.elasticity, which uses the opposite w.r.t.-tax-rate convention; the two are related by epsilon_taxrate = -(tau / (1 - tau)) * epsilon_netoftax.
population: Individuals with long-term capital gains in the United States
preferred_interpretation: Medium-run elasticity of long-term capital gains realizations with respect to the net-of-tax rate (1 - tau)
benchmark_summary: Matches the net-of-tax-rate convention used elsewhere in the registry for ETI, Frisch, Marshallian, and extensive-margin elasticities.
benchmark_source: Saez, Slemrod, and Giertz 2012; Auerbach 1988; capital-gains realizations literature

name: TFP persistence
description: AR(1) coefficient on total factor productivity in a log-linear macro process.
population: U.S. aggregate TFP process
preferred_interpretation: Quarterly or annual persistence coefficient, explicitly stating the frequency
benchmark_summary: A canonical macro-shock persistence parameter.
